programs.name: South Dakota Medicaid for Seniors/Disabled
programs.what_it_does: Provides medical coverage for eligible seniors and disabled individuals, including those 65 and older or blind/disabled under 65. Covers groups like SSI recipients and those in adult foster care or assisted living.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 65+, blind or disabled individuals under 65, former SSI recipients meeting income/resource limits (e.g., $2,000 resources).[1]
programs.benefit_value: Medical coverage including long-term services; resource limit $2,000 for individuals.[1]

programs.name: HOPE Waiver (Home & Community-Based Options and Person Centered Excellence)
programs.what_it_does: Offers home and community-based services to prevent institutionalization, including homemaker services, private duty nursing, adult day care, meals, assisted living, and structured family caregiving. Allows participants to live in their home or caregiver's home with support services.[1][2]
programs.who_its_for: Individuals 65+ or 18+ with disabilities at risk of institutionalization, meeting Medicaid income/resource eligibility; home equity limit $752,000.[1][2]
programs.benefit_value: Services like nursing, meals, emergency response, adult companion; may use Miller Trust for higher incomes.[1][2]

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: Not specifically named or detailed in South Dakota sources for seniors.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Not explicitly named in South Dakota sources; part of Medicaid ABD programs covering Medicare premiums/costs for low-income seniors.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries aged, blind, or disabled.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Pays Medicare premiums, deductibles, copays (varies by program).[2]

programs.name: SNAP/Food Assistance (Senior Box Program/CSFP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly pre-packaged boxes of nutritious, non-perishable food via USDA Commodity Supplemental Food Program to supplement diets. Administered statewide by Feeding South Dakota and SD Dept. of Education.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Monthly Senior Box of nutrient-supplementing foods.[3]

programs.name: Low Income Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Helps low-income South Dakotans pay home heating costs through Community Action Programs.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance
programs.what_it_does: Provides weatherization services through Community Action Programs to improve energy efficiency for low-income homes.

programs.name: Senior Health Information and Insurance Education (SHIINE)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free, unbiased, confidential counseling on Medicare, insurance, and health options. Funded federally for senior health insurance education.[7]

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Not explicitly detailed; nutritional meals provided via programs like HOPE Waiver or senior food assistance.

programs.name: Respite Care/Caregiver Support (HOPE Waiver & Family Support 360)
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite care, homemaking, personal hygiene via HOPE Waiver; FS 360 coordinates family/community supports for caregivers.
programs.who_its_for: Caregivers of 65+ or disabled 18+; families needing support.[6]

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Senior Citizen Handbook covers consumer protection and legal resources; helpline for scams/financial crimes targeting elderly.[5]

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman
programs.what_it_does: Not explicitly detailed in sources; part of LTSS oversight via DHS/DSS.

programs.name: Senior Companions of South Dakota
programs.what_it_does: This program matches healthy senior volunteers aged 55+ with older adults and individuals 21+ needing assistance to live independently. Volunteers provide free companionship, light housekeeping, meal planning, transportation, and social activities to reduce isolation and delay long-term care.[1][6]
programs.who_its_for: Older adults and adults 21+ needing help to remain independent, especially in rural areas; no charge to clients.[1]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific volunteer matching program sponsored by Good Samaritan Society, emphasizing rural South Dakota communities with free peer companionship services not universally available nationwide.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Free services including 10-40 hours/week of volunteer assistance per client; prevents long-term care placement and reduces stress.[1]

programs.name: 60's Plus Dining
programs.what_it_does: This program delivers nutritious meals through congregate dining, home delivery, star cards, and frozen meal packs to prevent malnutrition and isolation. It serves rural food desert areas with options like 7-pack frozen meals including milk and juice on a donation basis.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Anyone aged 60+ and their spouses, regardless of income or health; over 3,000 clients in 10 east-central SD counties.[4]
programs.whats_unique: No income guidelines for participation, with emphasis on rural SD food deserts via frozen meal packs; operated by Inter-Lakes Community Action Partnership since 1973.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Free or donation-based meals (suggested $35 for 7-pack); multiple formats to suit mobility and location needs.[4]

programs.name: Senior Box Program (CSFP)
programs.what_it_does: Administered statewide by Feeding South Dakota and SD Department of Education, it provides monthly pre-packaged boxes of nutrient-rich non-perishable foods. This supplements diets for low-income seniors, freeing funds for medicine and utilities.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 60+ with limited income in all 66 SD counties.[3]
programs.whats_unique: Statewide partnership delivering USDA CSFP boxes tailored to SD's rural seniors facing food insecurity; covers every county.[3]
programs.benefit_value: One free nutrient-supplementing food box per month per eligible senior.[3]
